note_4_13_0.js.title: Notation Trap: Kc vs Kp Brackets
note_4_13_0.js.text: For Kc expressions, ALWAYS use square brackets [ ]. Examiners will not award notation marks if you use round brackets. This is different from Kp which MUST use round brackets p(...).

note_4_13_0.js.title: Critical Misconception: Kc ≠ the Equilibrium Position
note_4_13_0.js.text: Kc is constant at a given temperature and does NOT change when you alter concentration, pressure, or add a catalyst. The position of equilibrium shifts to restore Qc = Kc, but Kc itself remains unchanged. Only temperature changes K.

note_4_13_1.js.title: Kp Notation — Most Penalised Error
note_4_13_1.js.text: For Kp expressions, ALWAYS use round brackets: p(CO₂), p(N₂). Do NOT use square brackets. Examiners explicitly withhold marks for incorrect bracket notation in IAL exams — this is ONE OF THE MOST PENALISED ERRORS.

note_4_13_1.js.title: Kp-Kc Relationship Trap
note_4_13_1.js.text: Remember Kp = Kc(RT)^Δn works only when R = 8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹ (NOT 8.31). Use wrong R value and your answer fails completely.

note_4_13_2.js.title: Stoichiometric Coefficients in RICE Tables
note_4_13_2.js.text: Students often forget to apply stoichiometric coefficients to changes: if the coefficient of NO₂ in 2NO₂ ⇌ N₂O₄ is 2, the change in NO₂ is TWICE the change in N₂O₄. Check your ICE table — each change must reflect stoichiometry.

note_4_13_2.js.title: % Reacted Calculations
note_4_13_2.js.text: If told 'X% of species A reacted', calculate change as (X/100) × initial moles, then use stoichiometric ratios for all other species. Students often mix percentages incorrectly.

note_4_13_3.js.title: Cause & Effect: The Critical Distinction (EXAM FOCUS)
note_4_13_3.js.text: Most students incorrectly say 'changing concentration changes Kc.' WRONG. Kc is constant. When you change concentration, Qc changes. The position of equilibrium SHIFTS to restore Qc = Kc. Kc is the cause; the shift is the effect.

note_4_13_3.js.title: Pressure Only Affects if Δn ≠ 0
note_4_13_3.js.text: Pressure changes have NO effect on equilibrium if the number of moles of gas is the same on both sides (Δn = 0). Many students forget this condition and incorrectly predict shifts for reactions like N₂ ⇌ N₂.

note_4_13_4.js.title: Temperature: The ONLY Factor That Changes K
note_4_13_4.js.text: Concentration, pressure, and catalysts shift equilibrium but do NOT change K. ONLY temperature changes K. For exothermic: increase T → K decreases, shift LEFT. For endothermic: increase T → K increases, shift RIGHT. Memorise this asymmetry.

note_4_13_4.js.title: Haber Process Example
note_4_13_4.js.text: N₂ + 3H₂ ⇌ 2NH₃ is exothermic (ΔH = −92 kJ). Low temperature favours products thermodynamically, but reaction is slow. Industry uses ~450 K and 200 atm as a compromise — high pressure shifts right, moderate temperature balances rate vs yield.

note_4_13_5.js.title: IAL Form: ΔStotal = R ln K (Not ΔG°)
note_4_13_5.js.text: In Edexcel IAL exams, the critical equation is ΔStotal = R ln K, not ΔG° = −RT ln K. Know both forms. Key implication: K > 1 → ln K > 0 → ΔStotal > 0 → spontaneous forward reaction.

note_4_13_5.js.title: Both Directions Spontaneous at Equilibrium
note_4_13_5.js.text: Students struggle to understand why both forward and reverse reactions are spontaneous at equilibrium. At equilibrium ΔStotal = 0, representing the entropy maximum. ANY deviation from equilibrium (starting from pure N₂O₄ or pure NO₂) spontaneously moves toward equilibrium because entropy increases.

note_4_13_6.js.title: Interpreting K Values for Extent of Reaction
note_4_13_6.js.text: K > 10³ = essentially complete conversion to products. K ≈ 1−10 = moderate conversion. K < 10⁻³ = negligible conversion. Use this to communicate exam answers: don't just state K value, interpret its magnitude.

note_4_13_6.js.title: Units of K Depend on Δn — Don't Compare Across Reactions
note_4_13_6.js.text: K = 100 mol dm⁻³ (units) is NOT necessarily 'stronger' than K = 2 (dimensionless) because their units differ. Always check Δn and units before comparing K values across different reactions.

note_4_13_7.js.title: Only Sparingly Soluble Compounds
note_4_13_7.js.text: Solubility products apply ONLY to sparingly soluble ionic compounds. You cannot effectively use Ksp for highly soluble salts like NaCl because ion interactions interfere with the simplified equilibrium model.

note_4_13_7.js.title: Why No Solid in Ksp
note_4_13_7.js.text: The concentration (density) of a pure solid is constant. It's incorporated into the Ksp value itself. This is why Ksp = [Ba²⁺][SO₄²⁻], NOT Ksp = [Ba²⁺][SO₄²⁻]/[BaSO₄].

note_4_13_8.js.title: Unit Conversion Essential
note_4_13_8.js.text: Solubility MUST be converted to mol dm⁻³ before calculating Ksp. If given in g dm⁻³, divide by molar mass first. Calculator error with scientific notation when squaring (3.42 × 10⁻⁴)² — always use brackets.

note_4_13_8.js.title: Reverse Ksp Calculations: 's' Variable Setup
note_4_13_8.js.text: Let solubility = s mol dm⁻³. For Mg(OH)₂, [Mg²⁺] = s, [OH⁻] = 2s. Substitute into Ksp = [Mg²⁺][OH⁻]² = s(2s)² = 4s³. Master this algebra — it appears frequently in exams.

note_4_13_9.js.title: Common Ion Effect: Le Chatelier Explanation Required
note_4_13_9.js.text: Adding NaCl to a PbCl₂ saturated solution increases [Cl⁻]. By Le Chatelier, equilibrium shifts LEFT to reduce this increase, forming more solid PbCl₂ and reducing its solubility. State both mechanism (Le Chatelier) and the direction (left) for full marks.

note_4_13_9.js.title: Common Ion Assumption in Calculations
note_4_13_9.js.text: In Ksp calculations with common ion, assume [Cl⁻] ≈ 0.100 mol dm⁻³ (from added salt) and ignore the tiny amount from PbCl₂ dissolution. This simplification is valid because Ksp is very small. If you do not make this assumption, you get a cubic equation beyond A-level scope.

note_4_14_0.js.title: Identifying Acid/Base: Sequential Steps Critical
note_4_14_0.js.text: Problem: Students identify acid/base randomly. Solution: (1) Write full equation. (2) Find species losing H⁺ → acid. (3) Find species gaining H⁺ → base. (4) Conjugate pairs differ by one H⁺. Follow these steps religiously.

note_4_14_0.js.title: Amphoteric Species Are Everywhere
note_4_14_0.js.text: Water, HCO₃⁻, HSO₄⁻, amino acids can all act as both acid and base. In an exam, if asked to identify the role of water in a reaction, always consider: is it donating H⁺ (acid) or accepting H⁺ (base)? Context matters.

note_4_14_1.js.title: Strong Acid → Weak Conjugate Base (Inverse Relationship)
note_4_14_1.js.text: HCl is a very strong acid; its conjugate base Cl⁻ is extremely weak (inert, does not accept H⁺). Conversely, CH₃COOH is weak; its conjugate base CH₃COO⁻ is relatively strong. This inverse relationship comes from equilibrium: if forward reaction heavily favours products, reverse does not.

note_4_14_1.js.title: Polyprotic Acids: HSO₄⁻ Appears Twice
note_4_14_1.js.text: In H₂SO₄ → HSO₄⁻ → SO₄²⁻, the HSO₄⁻ is BOTH the conjugate base of H₂SO₄ AND the conjugate acid of SO₄²⁻. This makes it amphoteric. Exams test this concept — students miss it if not careful.

note_4_14_2.js.title: Exam Definition of pH: Write the Expression, Not Words
note_4_14_2.js.text: If asked to define pH in an exam, write: pH = −log₁₀[H⁺]. Do NOT try to define it in words — examiners reject worded definitions if details are missed. The expression is unambiguous.

note_4_14_2.js.title: Negative pH is Real and Valid
note_4_14_2.js.text: pH = −log[H⁺]. If [H⁺] > 1 mol dm⁻³, then pH is negative (e.g., 2 mol dm⁻³ HCl gives pH = −0.30). In exams, negative pH answers are fully acceptable if calculations support them. Students often panic thinking it's an error.

note_4_14_3.js.title: Strength vs Concentration: Do NOT Confuse
note_4_14_3.js.text: Strength (strong/weak) = % of acid molecules that ionise. Concentration (concentrated/dilute) = total amount dissolved. You can have concentrated weak acid (glacial ethanoic acid, ~17 mol dm⁻³, barely ionised) or dilute strong acid (0.0001 M HCl, fully ionised). Examiners specifically mark confusion between these.

note_4_14_3.js.title: Degree of Dissociation α Increases with Dilution
note_4_14_3.js.text: Dilution shifts weak acid equilibrium right (Le Chatelier), increasing α. A 0.01 M weak acid has higher α than 0.1 M. This means dilute weak acids are actually more dissociated (by percentage) — counterintuitive but crucial.

note_4_14_4.js.title: Two Assumptions for Weak Acid Simplification (EXAM FAVOURITE)
note_4_14_4.js.text: Assumption 1: [HA]eq ≈ [HA]initial (because dissociation is < 5%). Assumption 2: All H⁺ comes from the weak acid (water contribution negligible). BOTH must be verified. After solving [H⁺] = √(Ka × c), CHECK: [H⁺]/c × 100 < 5%. If not, solve the quadratic.

note_4_14_4.js.title: No Approximation Allowed if Non-Weak Acid
note_4_14_4.js.text: If Ka > 10⁻⁴ or concentration < 0.01 M, the approximation fails. You must solve the quadratic Ka = x²/(c−x) without simplification. Exams test this — students blindly use √(Ka×c) and get marked down.

note_4_14_5.js.title: Exam Language Trap: 'Minimises' NOT 'Prevents'
note_4_14_5.js.text: Incorrect: 'A buffer prevents pH change.' Correct: 'A buffer minimises (or resists / keeps roughly constant) significant changes in pH.' Examiners deduct marks for 'prevents' — the pH DOES change, just by very little.

note_4_14_5.js.title: Buffer Mechanism: Le Chatelier in Action
note_4_14_5.js.text: If acid (H⁺) is added → A⁻ + H⁺ → HA consumes the added H⁺. If base (OH⁻) is added → HA + OH⁻ → A⁻ + H₂O consumes the base. The buffer works by having large reservoirs of BOTH HA and A⁻ to neutralise small additions of acid or base.

note_4_14_6.js.title: Equivalence ≠ pH 7 (Except Strong+Strong)
note_4_14_6.js.text: Strong acid + strong base → equivalence at pH 7. Weak acid + strong base → equivalence at pH > 7 (salt is weak base). Strong acid + weak base → equivalence at pH < 7 (salt is weak acid). Weak+weak → NO sharp equivalence point. Exams test selection of wrong indicator.

note_4_14_6.js.title: Indicator Selection: Match to Steep Region
note_4_14_6.js.text: For weak acid + strong base (equivalence ~pH 9), use phenolphthalein (pH 8.3–10.0), NOT methyl orange (pH 2.7–4.7). Methyl orange changes colour way below the steep section where it's useless. Students pick indicators by name, not pH range — leading mistake.

note_4_14_7.js.title: Half-Neutralisation: [HA] = [A⁻] → Ka = [H⁺]
note_4_14_7.js.text: At half-neutralisation, exactly half the acid is converted to salt. [HA] = [A⁻]. From Ka = [H⁺][A⁻]/[HA], the [A⁻]/[HA] term = 1, so Ka = [H⁺]. Therefore [H⁺] = Ka, and Ka can be calculated directly from pH measurement. This is the trick to CP11.

note_4_14_7.js.title: CP11 Method: Add Acid After Titration
note_4_14_7.js.text: Titrate to equivalence with NaOH (equivalence volume = V_eq noted). Then pipette a FURTHER 25 cm³ of the ORIGINAL ethanoic acid into the same flask. At this point, [HA] = [A⁻] because exactly half the original acid is now unreacted, and half is neutralised salt. Measure pH → Ka = 10^(−pH).

note_4_15_0.js.title: Chiral Centre: Four DIFFERENT Groups Required
note_4_15_0.js.text: 99% of student errors: identifying chiral centres with identical groups. If two groups are the same (e.g., two CH₃ groups), the carbon is NOT chiral, even if bonded to four different elements. Check for identical groups carefully — this is THE most common mistake.

note_4_15_0.js.title: Wedge/Hash Bonds Must Show 3D Arrangement
note_4_15_0.js.text: Wedge (▲) = coming out of paper. Hash (⋯) = going into paper. These MUST be drawn at chiral centres to show 3D arrangement, or you cannot identify enantiomers. Students draw flat structures and miss stereochemistry entirely.

note_4_15_1.js.title: Racemate Exam Definition: Equimolar 50:50
note_4_15_1.js.text: Exam definition: 'A racemic mixture is an equimolar (50:50) mixture of the two enantiomers.' You must use the word 'equimolar' or 'equal amounts' — vague definitions lose marks. Shows NO optical activity in polarimeter because rotations cancel.

note_4_15_1.js.title: Thalidomide: Drug Chirality Matters (Clinical Importance)
note_4_15_1.js.text: Thalidomide was sold as a racemate. One enantiomer stopped nausea (therapeutic). The other caused severe birth defects (teratogenic). Taken in first trimester (when limbs form), it caused phocomelia. This tragedy led to strict pharmaceutical regulations requiring separate testing of each enantiomer. Exams ask about this.

note_4_15_2.js.title: SN1 Produces Racemic Mixture (Planar Intermediates)
note_4_15_2.js.text: SN1: carbocation intermediate is sp² trigonal planar. Nucleophile attacks equally from above or below → 50% (R) and 50% (S) produced. Starting with pure enantiomer, product shows ZERO optical rotation (racemic). This is diagnostic for SN1.

note_4_15_2.js.title: SN2 Produces Inversion (Backside Attack+Umbrella Flip)
note_4_15_2.js.text: SN2: nucleophile attacks from opposite side of leaving group (backside). Causes three remaining groups to flip like an umbrella (inversion of configuration). (R) → (S) or vice versa. Optical rotation is equal magnitude but OPPOSITE direction. This proves SN2.

note_4_15_3.js.title: Aldehyde vs Ketone Reactivity Difference
note_4_15_3.js.text: Aldehydes are more reactive than ketones in nucleophilic addition because the aldehyde carbon has only one alkyl group (less steric bulk). Ketone carbon has two alkyl groups (more hindered). This difference is exploited in chemical tests: Tollens and Fehling only oxidise aldehydes under mild conditions.

note_4_15_3.js.title: Functional Group Priority in Naming
note_4_15_3.js.text: If molecule contains both CHO (aldehyde) and C=O (ketone), name it as an aldehyde (higher priority). The ketone becomes an 'oxo–' prefix. Always check priority: carboxylic acid > aldehyde > ketone > alcohol.

note_4_15_4.js.title: A-Level Simplification: Use H⁻ Notation (Not BH₄⁻)
note_4_15_4.js.text: The real nucleophile in NaBH₄ reduction is the BH₄⁻ ion, but IAL exams expect the simplified mechanism using H⁻. Write H⁻ with its lone pair above the symbol (: H⁻). This is a deliberate simplification to avoid boron chemistry beyond scope. NEVER write H⁻ without the lone pair.

note_4_15_4.js.title: Two-Step Workup: Complex Must Be Broken Down
note_4_15_4.js.text: After NaBH₄ reduction, a boron-containing complex forms. Step 2 requires adding water or dilute acid to release the free alcohol. Without this workup, you cannot isolate the product. Exam questions often specify '(i) NaBH₄ (ii) water workup' — both steps essential.

note_4_15_5.js.title: Carboxylate Ion: Charge Delocalised Over Two C–O Bonds
note_4_15_5.js.text: RCOOH loses H⁺ → RCOO⁻. The negative charge is NOT localised on one oxygen. It spreads equally (resonance) over both C–O bonds (shown as dashed lines). This stabilisation makes carboxylic acids much stronger acids than alcohols (pKa ~4 vs ~16). Resonance delocalisation is the KEY.

note_4_15_5.js.title: COOH Carbon ALWAYS C1 in Numbering
note_4_15_5.js.text: In nomenclature, the carboxylic acid carbon is always position 1, even if there are double bonds or other functional groups. Students often miscount — this costs marks. 3-methylbutanoic acid has COOH at C1, methyl at C3 of the chain.

note_4_15_6.js.title: Acyl Chlorides: Both Carbonyls AND Haloalkane Properties
note_4_15_6.js.text: Acyl chlorides (RCOCl) are NOT simple carbonyls. The carbonyl carbon is bonded to BOTH δ⁻ oxygen AND δ⁻ chlorine → extremely δ⁺ carbon. This makes them FAR more reactive than aldehydes/ketones. They react at room temperature without catalyst. Students incorrectly apply carbonyl chemistry — acyl chlorides are in a different reactivity league.

note_4_15_6.js.title: Nucleophile MUST Have H on Attacking Atom
note_4_15_6.js.text: Nucleophiles that react with acyl chlorides must be H–O–R (alcohol), H–N–R (amine), or H₂O. The H is critical — it's removed in the final elimination step. Tertiary amines (R₃N) do NOT react because they have no H on N. Exam trick question: which amine reacts? Answer: primary/secondary only.

note_4_15_7.js.title: Paper Chromatography: Stationary Phase is WATER IN PAPER
note_4_15_7.js.text: THE most common exam trap: 'What is the stationary phase?' Answer: the water trapped within the fibres of the cellulose paper, NOT the paper itself. The paper is just a support. Mobile phase = the solvent travelling up. This distinction appears in mark schemes.

note_4_15_7.js.title: TLC Separation Via Adsorption (Not Partition)
note_4_15_7.js.text: Paper uses partition (polar water vs non-polar solvent). TLC uses adsorption: silica gel has −OH groups that are highly polar. Polar compounds stick to silica (low Rf). Non-polar compounds stay dissolved in solvent (high Rf). Different mechanism, different principle.

note_4_15_8.js.title: NMR δ Axis Increases Right-to-LEFT (Opposite of Normal)
note_4_15_8.js.text: Unlike normal graphs, the NMR δ axis increases from RIGHT (δ = 0) to LEFT (δ = 200+). Downfield (deshielded) is LEFT. Upfield (shielded) is RIGHT. Students often reverse this and misinterpret spectra. Memorise: LEFT = downfield = high δ = deshielded (electrons pulled away).

note_4_15_8.js.title: TMS Reference Peak at δ = 0
note_4_15_8.js.text: TMS (tetramethylsilane) is the universal reference standard for NMR. Appears at δ = 0 on all ¹H and ¹³C spectra. If you see a peak at the far right edge (δ ≈ 0), that's TMS — not part of your sample. Used to calibrate all other signals.
